front: What is the tradeoff between Copy-on-Write (CoW) and Merge-on-Read (MoR) in Iceberg?
back: Copy-on-Write (CoW): when a row is updated/deleted, Iceberg rewrites the entire data file containing that row without the old version. Write amplification is high (rewriting potentially large files for a few row changes), but reads are simple — no delete files to merge, just read the data file. Optimal when reads are far more frequent than writes and query performance is paramount. Merge-on-Read (MoR): row-level changes write small delete files (positional or equality delete files) without touching original data files. Writes are fast and cheap; reads are slower because the engine must merge data files with delete files on-the-fly. Delete files accumulate over time and degrade read performance, requiring periodic compaction. Optimal for high write throughput use cases (streaming CDC ingestion, frequent updates). The production pattern: use MoR for streaming writes, then run compaction jobs during low-traffic windows to convert delete files into CoW-style clean data files, restoring read performance. This gives you fast writes AND fast reads at the cost of operational complexity.
